title: China’s moment to shape a new global order
gist: MERICS argues Beijing is exploiting Trump's unilateralism and back-to-back Trump/Putin summits to seize agenda-setting power over the US-China relationship and to advance a China-led alternative to the liberal international order, though its selective adherence to international rules leaves its legitimacy unproven.

themes.theme: China's bid to reset the US-China relationship on its terms

insights.claim: Beijing is reframing the US-China relationship around 'managed competition' rather than confrontation, seeking to reduce tensions while avoiding major concessions on trade or Taiwan.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Signals a lower-volatility but not conciliatory China posture; de-escalation without structural concessions means core disputes remain unresolved.
insights.locator: China is seeking to reduce tensions with the US

insights.claim: The US adopted China's own wording of 'constructive strategic stability' even without explicitly endorsing the concept, showing agenda-setting power over the bilateral relationship has shifted toward Beijing.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Control of the diplomatic vocabulary is a leading indicator of shifting power; watch which side's framing terms get adopted.
insights.quote: the US may not have explicitly endorsed the idea of “constructive strategic stability,” but it did adopt China’s wording
insights.locator: Defining the terms of engagement with the US

insights.claim: The Chinese Communist Party's growing confidence reflects a view that its authoritarian system is not inferior to Western liberal democracy and may be superior.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Ideological confidence, not just tactics, is driving Beijing's global posture, implying a durable rather than opportunistic challenge to the liberal order.
insights.quote: the authoritarian political system is not inferior to the Western liberal democracy – and may quite possibly even be superior
insights.locator: This reflects the Chinese Communist Party’s growing confidence

themes.theme: Trump's unilateralism as an accelerant for China

insights.claim: Trump's 'America First' unilateralism has pushed cautious Western allies (German, French, British, Canadian leaders) to visit Beijing, creating a more favorable geopolitical environment for China.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: US retrenchment is a tailwind for Chinese influence; the trend of Western leaders courting Beijing is a tradeable signal of realignment.
insights.quote: Trump’s unilateralism has even encouraged US allies to adjust long cautious and confrontational approaches to China.
insights.locator: This is not Beijing’s achievement alone.

insights.claim: China's regional standing improved with Modi's attendance at the 2025 SCO summit and the signing of the ASEAN-China Free Trade Area 3.0 upgrade protocol.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Concrete markers of China consolidating regional economic and diplomatic ties as US influence recedes in Asia.
insights.locator: China’s relations with its neighboring countries

themes.theme: Mutual dependence and leverage among great powers

insights.claim: Both Moscow and Washington currently need China: Putin needs China's economic lifeline to sustain the Ukraine war, while the US relies on Chinese rare earth supplies, its agricultural export market, and Beijing's restraint of Iran and stability in the Taiwan Strait.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: China's centrality to both rivals is a structural source of leverage; rare earth and agricultural trade are the concrete pressure points to watch.
insights.quote: Moscow and Washington currently both need China, albeit in very different ways.
insights.locator: In an advantageous position to advance its geopolitical vision

insights.claim: Xi drew a red line on Taiwan by calling it the 'most important issue,' and Trump appeared to accommodate it by stating after his Beijing visit that he is 'not looking to have somebody go independent.'
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: A possible softening of US commitment to Taiwan; monitor US rhetoric on Taiwanese independence as a geopolitical risk barometer.
insights.quote: I’m not looking to have somebody go independent

themes.theme: The legitimacy gap in China's alternative order

insights.claim: The China-Russia joint declaration on a 'new type of international relations' calls for 'equal and indivisible security' and 'democratization of international relations,' framing an alternative to the US-led liberal order.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Codifies the ideological blueprint of the China-Russia axis; the language ('indivisible security') is used to legitimize spheres of influence.
insights.quote: rules set by a minority of states cannot replace commonly accepted international norms

insights.claim: China's selective adherence to international rules (rejecting the 2016 UNCLOS tribunal ruling on the South China Sea, Russia's security-at-Ukraine's-expense) risks turning its multilateralism into a vehicle to compete with the US rather than constrain great-power rivalry.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: The core weakness in China's pitch: it risks reproducing the hegemony it claims to replace, capping the durability of its coalition.
insights.quote: China risks reproducing elements of the US-led system it wants to replace
insights.locator: This overlooks Russia’s pursuit of security

insights.claim: China may currently be benefiting only from other countries' short-term hedging against a perceived US retreat, and its order-shaping influence will hold only if it provides genuine public goods and exercises restraint.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: China's ascendancy may be fragile and reversible; a US re-engagement or Chinese overreach could unwind the current favorable environment.
insights.quote: it may simply be benefiting from other countries’ short-term efforts to hedge against a perceived retreat of the US from global leadership
insights.locator: China is making use of a favorable international environment
